next_steps: Inspect U-M digital-library administrative metadata or ask the repository whether the carrier barcode and complete current image route can be supplied.
notes: Until resolved, the U-M page-image entity remains distinct from the Boston Public Library/Internet Archive facsimile.
openQuestions.question: Which physical copy supplied U-M's 234 historical page images, and is a currently auditable complete image route available?

next_steps: Retry the exact official PDF endpoint later or ask Trinity College/Bodleian Libraries for a supported direct copy if the verification challenge persists.
notes: No local copy is recorded because the current response was HTML/403 rather than a verified PDF.
openQuestions.question: Can the exact official Trinity College Oxford MS 57 catalog PDF be retrieved through a repository-supported route without bypassing the current verification challenge?

next_steps: Search personal-paper finding aids and correspondence catalogs for the named scholars, then contact plausible repositories if item-level records remain unavailable.
notes: Their intermediary role is explicit in the edition even though current repositories are unresolved.
openQuestions.question: Where do the Lucy Toulmin Smith, Eugen Kölbing, and F. J. Furnivall working transcripts now survive, if at all?

next_steps: Collate the printed text and apparatus against complete images or diplomatic transcriptions of both principal witnesses.
notes: Both principal transcription routes are secure; only their exact reading partition is unresolved.
openQuestions.question: What exact line-by-line partition of Horstmann's Roberd of Cisyle derives from Vernon versus Trinity College Oxford MS 57?

next_steps: Inspect a complete Arundel MS 168 facsimile or a more granular item-level catalog description.
notes: The source witness is confirmed; only the endpoint is inferred from Dorothe's cataloged start at f. 5r.
openQuestions.question: Can Cristine's inferred Arundel MS 168 endpoint at f. 4v be confirmed directly?

next_steps: Trace historical Cambridge shelfmark forms in nineteenth-century catalogs and compare Horstmann's description with the James record.
notes: Resolution would improve identifier history but would not add the witness to the 1878 edition source chain.
openQuestions.question: Can Horstmann's unavailable Cbr. Ji IV, 8 reference be proven to be current CUL MS Ii.4.9 rather than merely identified with high probability?

next_steps: Seek an explicit repository or publisher statement defining the licensed material scope.
notes: The manifest records the repository statement but leaves the artifact's item-level copyright status unknown.
openQuestions.question: Does the CME repository's Unlicense file intentionally apply to the corpus XML/data as well as software?

reviewNotes: The cataloging subject is the complete 1878 edited selection: eighteen count-bearing components and twenty-two serialized streams.
No abstract-work entity is minted for each component; component identity and metrics live in work metadata, while relations are scoped to component labels and exact ranges.
Direct and mediated editorial routes are separated. No direct Horstmann-to-codex edge is asserted where Laing or a working transcript supplied the reading.
CUL Ii.4.9 is retained only to resolve the printed Cbr. Ji IV, 8 reference and is explicitly excluded from the 1878 source chain because Horstmann says it was unavailable.

Every cached object states target-work presence. The eight approved representation groups account for ten present local copies; the other twenty-seven local copies are absent and have no work_portion.
The older Auchinleck project is a 701-image reconstruction spanning the NLS codex and detached Edinburgh fragments, not one fictional physical carrier.
No death date is asserted for the correct Carl Horstmann authority.

lineage.summary: The pinned General Corpus XML serializes U-M electronic item AJD8171.0001.001, an uncorrected-OCR/TEI representation of Carl Horstmann's 1878 eighteen-component anthology. Horstmann transcribed or collated fifteen physical manuscript witnesses, sometimes through Laing's print or working transcripts by Lucy Toulmin Smith, Eugen Kölbing, and F. J. Furnivall. Four components print two version streams. This graph keeps the unavailable sixteenth manuscript reference (CUL Ii.4.9), U-M page-image production, the collateral Boston Public Library scan, complete manuscript facsimiles, detached Auchinleck fragments, catalogs, rights statements, and repository artifact distinct.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Middle English authors and translators; William Paris (Cristine)
catalogSummary.date: Medieval component texts compiled and first published as this scholarly selection in 1878
catalogSummary.title: Sammlung altenglischer Legenden

catalogingSubject.label: Sammlung altenglischer Legenden
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The complete eighteen-component scholarly anthology compiled and edited by Carl Horstmann and published in 1878: seven components in the Vernon collection and eleven Einzellegenden, including the prose Adam-and-Eve appendix. It is not one medieval work, one manuscript, the 1881 second series, or twenty-two independent works. Four components serialize two witness-version streams; witnesses, transcripts, print intermediaries, facsimiles, the U-M encoding, and the repository XML remain separate lineage layers.

contentParts.label: Part I: Vernon collection
contentParts.summary: A seven-component sequence transcribed from the Vernon manuscript and compared with corresponding Legenda aurea chapters.

contentParts.label: S. Paula
notes: Three prose P elements are German editorial argument, not prose-form content of the item.
contentParts.summary: A metrical legendary account of Saint Paula within the Vernon sequence.

contentParts.label: S. Ambrosius
contentParts.summary: A metrical life of Saint Ambrose, including the nested Theodosius episode.

contentParts.label: De quadam virgine in Antiochia
contentParts.summary: The legend of a maiden of Antioch followed by the Sithia and Climonen continuation.

contentParts.label: De S. Theodora
contentParts.summary: A metrical legend of Saint Theodora.

contentParts.label: S. Bernard
contentParts.summary: A metrical life of Saint Bernard.

contentParts.label: S. Augustin
contentParts.summary: A metrical life of Saint Augustine.

contentParts.label: Savinian and Savina
contentParts.summary: A metrical legend of Savinian and Savina.

contentParts.label: Part II: Einzellegenden
contentParts.summary: Eleven individual legendary, biblical, debate, romance, and appendix components.

contentParts.label: Kindheit Jesu
notes: One item/form count is owned here; the two zero-item children alone own the 137 groups and 1,538 lines.
contentParts.summary: Two serialized Middle English versions of narratives about Christ's childhood.

contentParts.label: Kindheit Jesu: Harley MS 3954 version
contentParts.summary: The first serialized witness version.

contentParts.label: Kindheit Jesu: Harley MS 2399 version
contentParts.summary: The second serialized witness version.

contentParts.label: Canticum de Creatione
notes: One item/form count is owned here; the two zero-item children alone own the 202 groups and 1,983 lines.
contentParts.summary: Two serialized creation narratives: the Trinity version and a composite Auchinleck stream surviving in Edinburgh and NLS fragments.

contentParts.label: Canticum: Trinity College Oxford MS 57 version
notes: Horstmann's fol. 156 citation is retained in lineage as historical foliation; the current catalog starts the item at fol. 157v.
contentParts.summary: The Trinity version, whose manuscript colophon dates the composition to 1375.

contentParts.label: Canticum: composite Auchinleck version
notes: The Horstmann stream reaches EUL MS 218 through Laing's print and reaches the NLS fragment through Lucy Toulmin Smith's transcript; it is not evidence of direct Horstmann autopsy of both carriers.
contentParts.summary: One continuous version reconstructed from the detached EUL fragment for lines 1–352 and the NLS fragment for lines 353–end.

contentParts.label: Magdalena
notes: One item/form count is owned here; the two zero-item children alone own the 3 groups and 1,325 lines. The prose P is an editorial loss note, not prose-form target content.
contentParts.summary: Two serialized Middle English versions of the life of Mary Magdalene.

contentParts.label: Magdalena: Laud Misc. 108 version
contentParts.summary: The first serialized version, directly transcribed from Laud Misc. 108.

contentParts.label: Magdalena: Auchinleck version
contentParts.summary: The second serialized version, mediated through Lucy Toulmin Smith's transcript with Kölbing's transcript consulted.

contentParts.label: Marina
contentParts.summary: A metrical legend of Saint Marina.

contentParts.label: Eufrosyne
contentParts.summary: A metrical legend of Saint Euphrosyne.

contentParts.label: Cristine
notes: The current catalog gives the f. 2 start; the f. 4v endpoint is inferred from Dorothe beginning at f. 5r.
contentParts.summary: William Paris's metrical life of Saint Christine.

contentParts.label: Dorothe
contentParts.summary: A metrical legend of Saint Dorothy with Latin verses and two prose prayers; the final English lines are supplied from Arundel 168 after a Harley leaf loss.

contentParts.label: Erasmus
notes: One item/form count is owned here; the two zero-item children alone own the 6 groups and 224 lines.
contentParts.summary: Two serialized versions of the Saint Erasmus legend; the Bedford stream continues from verse into a prose/list and prayer context.

contentParts.label: Erasmus: Harley MS 2382 version
contentParts.summary: The first serialized Erasmus version.

contentParts.label: Erasmus: Bedford manuscript version
contentParts.summary: The second serialized Erasmus version: fifty verse lines followed by list and prayer material.

contentParts.label: A disputisoun bytwene a cristenemon and a Jew
contentParts.summary: A hostile Christian-Jewish disputation structured around Sir Walter's victory over a Jewish opponent.

contentParts.label: Roberd of Cisyle
notes: Harley 1701, Harley 525, and CUL Ff.2.38 are collated witnesses. The unavailable CUL Ii.4.9 copy is not a source relation for this 1878 edition.
contentParts.summary: An eclectic edition of the Middle English romance in which a proud king is displaced, humbled, and restored.

contentParts.label: Beilage zur Creatio mundi: þe lyff of Adam and Eue
notes: The XML contains fifteen content P elements. Horstmann received the text through a Furnivall working transcript rather than claiming direct autopsy of Vernon at this locus.
contentParts.summary: A prose appendix recounting the creation and the life and sufferings of Adam and Eve.

notes: This dates the modern selection and edition, not the composition of all medieval components.

extent.description: Eighteen count-bearing editorial components: seven in Part I and eleven in Part II. Four components have two serialized version children, producing twenty-two text streams and twenty-eight modeled part records when the two zero-count containers and eight version children are included. XML metrics are 670 LG elements and 13,275 L elements; Part I contributes 190/5,658 and Part II 480/7,617.

formStatements.description: Fifteen verse-only items, two mixed verse-and-prose items, and one prose-only appendix.

scope.description: Horstmann's headings, introductions, arguments, and apparatus retained by the edition and encoding
Latin comparison text, rubrics, verses, prayers, and list material printed within the anthology

notes: The two part containers, eight version children, and eighteen component parents are explicit accounting layers; only the component parents contribute item/form counts.
The XML's dedication and table-of-contents DIV1 milestones are representation-only and do not become content parts.
The 1881 second series is outside this cataloging boundary.
The raw header language code ger is retained as source history but does not describe the Middle English component texts adequately.

next_steps: Add only component-scoped claims from current specialist scholarship; do not project witness or edition dates onto the whole selection.
openQuestions.question: What are the secure individual composition dates, dialect regions, and authorships of all eighteen medieval components?

next_steps: Request or inspect ff. 2r–5r and retain f. 4v as an inference until the physical boundary is visible.
openQuestions.question: Can direct Arundel MS 168 images or a component catalog explicitly confirm Cristine's inferred endpoint at fol. 4v?

next_steps: Collate every edition line against both witnesses before replacing the current explicitly unresolved dual-base scope with line-level source ranges.
openQuestions.question: What exact reading-level partition did Horstmann take from Vernon and Trinity MS 57 in the eclectic Roberd of Cisyle text?

notes: This catalog-facing rollup includes the sole named component author but does not make the anthology an authorial medieval collection or assign Horstmann authorship.

scope.description: The heterogeneous medieval English component texts, except for the component-specific William Paris attribution
Components explicitly presented as vernacular translations or adaptations of Latin legendary material
The 1878 anthology, its transcriptions, comparisons, supplied readings, and apparatus
Selection and organization of the eighteen editorial components

subjects.label: Creation, cosmology, and the natural world
Christian anti-Judaism, antisemitism, and anti-Jewish violence
notes: Records hostile Christian representation within the text rather than endorsing it.

scope.description: A broad anthology-level subject, with different emphases in individual components

summaries.value: Carl Horstmann's 1878 anthology presents eighteen Middle English religious and legendary components. Part I transcribes a seven-item Vernon-Manuscript sequence related to the Legenda aurea; Part II prints individual saints' lives, biblical and apocryphal narratives, a Christian-Jewish disputation, the romance of Roberd of Cisyle, and a prose Adam-and-Eve appendix. Four components preserve two serialized witness versions, while German editorial framing and Latin comparisons, rubrics, verses, and prayers remain part of the scholarly presentation.

Part I: Paula; Ambrosius; the maiden of Antioch; Theodora; Bernard; Augustine; Savinian and Savina. Part II: Kindheit Jesu; Canticum de Creatione; Magdalena; Marina; Eufrosyne; Cristine; Dorothe; Erasmus; A disputisoun bytwene a cristenemon and a Jew; Roberd of Cisyle; appendix on Adam and Eve.

The record counts eighteen editorial components once. Kindheit Jesu, Canticum de Creatione, Magdalena, and Erasmus each own one item/form count but no line metrics; their eight zero-item version children partition those components' 348 verse groups and 5,070 lines. Together with the seven single-stream Part II components, they produce the Part II total of 480 groups and 7,617 lines. The whole encoding serializes twenty-two streams but does not contain twenty-two independent works.

notes: Retains the historical title-page spelling rather than silently modernizing it.

work.summary: Carl Horstmann's 1878 anthology presents eighteen Middle English religious and legendary components. Part I transcribes a seven-item Vernon-Manuscript sequence related to the Legenda aurea; Part II prints individual saints' lives, biblical and apocryphal narratives, a Christian-Jewish disputation, the romance of Roberd of Cisyle, and a prose Adam-and-Eve appendix. Four components preserve two serialized witness versions, while German editorial framing and Latin comparisons, rubrics, verses, and prayers remain part of the scholarly presentation.
